summary: Spolsky draws parallels between the PC software era of the 1980s and the state of web development in 2007, identifying three recurring dynamics: hardware constraints that developers waste time optimizing for, portability problems across platforms, and the lack of standardized UI conventions. He argues that just as DOS applications were made obsolete by Windows providing a standard SDK, Ajax web applications will eventually be supplanted by a new SDK that provides a portable compiled-to-JavaScript language, standardized UI components, and cross-application interoperability. Using Lotus 1-2-3's loss to Microsoft Excel as a cautionary tale, he warns that dominant web apps like Gmail could become legacy software if they fail to adopt whatever new platform SDK wins developer mindshare.
key_points: Developers who waste effort optimizing for current hardware constraints (bandwidth, JS compile time) will lose to those who focus on features, because constraints always disappear with time

Browser incompatibilities will be solved not by sandboxes (which are slow and limited) but by a new portable language that compiles to native JavaScript for each browser, analogous to how C solved portability across instruction sets
Ajax applications lack standardized UI conventions and interoperability (like cut-and-paste between apps), mirroring the pre-Windows era of inconsistent DOS applications
Whichever SDK provides both a portable language and standardized UI/interop features will gain the same competitive stronghold that Microsoft Windows had through its API
Dominant incumbents like Google Gmail are vulnerable to displacement by a new platform SDK, exactly as Lotus 1-2-3 was displaced by Windows-based Excel
money_quotes: The developers who ignored performance and blasted ahead adding cool features to their applications will, in the long run, have better applications.
Sandboxes are penalty boxes; they're slow and they suck, which is why Java Applets are dead, dead, dead.

And while you're not paying attention, everybody starts writing NewSDK apps, and they're really good, and suddenly businesses ONLY want NewSDK apps, and all those old-school Plain Ajax apps look pathetic.
The NewSDK will be the second coming of Microsoft Windows; this is exactly how Lotus lost control of the spreadsheet market.
Their nail polish has more than 2GB.
And you laugh at them, for their NewSDK is a honking 232 megabytes of JavaScript, and it takes 76 seconds to load a page.
key_insight: History shows that platform shifts happen when someone builds a standard SDK that solves portability and UI consistency, and today's dominant web apps will be displaced just as DOS-era software was displaced by Windows — the only unknown is who builds it.
